companyCaseStudy.title: How GitHub Migrated Huge MySQL Tables Without Downtime (hundreds of millions of rows → <200ms locks)
companyCaseStudy.summary: When GitHub needed to change schemas on tables with hundreds of millions of rows, traditional ALTER TABLE meant minutes-to-hours of locks and replication pain. They built and open‑sourced gh-ost — an online schema migration tool — that turned painful, risky migrations into safe background jobs that finish with an atomic rename and no user-visible downtime.
companyCaseStudy.explanation:
## Hook
GitHub operates at massive scale: tables with hundreds of millions of rows powering issues, pull requests, and events. A single ALTER TABLE on those tables could block writes for minutes or hours and spike replication lag — a major reliability risk. The problem: how to change schema without stopping traffic.

## The Problem
- Massive tables ("tens to hundreds of millions" of rows) needed schema changes (add column, index changes, type changes).
- ALTER TABLE on InnoDB is blocking or requires long metadata locks.
- Blocking caused write stalls, replication lag spikes, and operational headaches during deploy windows.

### Why a naive approach failed
- COPY + rename on master: produced long locks or huge replication lag when replaying big changes.
- Pausing traffic or scheduling long maintenance windows: unacceptable for a global platform.

## The Challenge
Make schema changes that:
- Cause no user-visible downtime
- Avoid long blocking locks on the primary
- Keep replication lag bounded
- Let operators monitor and throttle progress safely

## The Solution — gh-ost (ghost tables + binlog-driven diff)
GitHub developed gh-ost (open-sourced) to perform online schema migrations. High-level flow:

```mermaid
graph LR
  App[Application] --> Primary[MySQL Primary]
  Primary --> Replicas[Replicas]
  gh-ost --> Primary
  gh-ost --> Replicas
  gh-ost --> GhostTable[Ghost Table]
  GhostTable --> Swap[Atomic RENAME]
  Swap --> Primary
  note right of gh-ost: Reads binlog, applies
```

How it works (story form):
1. gh-ost creates a ghost table with the desired schema on the same database.
2. It copies data in chunks from the original table into the ghost table while throttling to avoid Replica lag.
3. It tails the binlog (or uses replicas) to apply live changes (inserts/updates/deletes) to the ghost table so it stays in sync.
4. When copy + applier are caught up, gh-ost performs a very fast, atomic table swap using RENAME TABLE (milliseconds lock window) and drops the old table.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A[Start migration] --> B[Create ghost table]
  B --> C[Chunked copy of existing rows]
  C --> D[Binlog applier keeps ghost synced]
  D --> E[Catch up & validate]
  E --> F[Atomic RENAME swap]
  F --> G[Done — old table dropped]
```

### Key operational features
- Chunked copy with throttling: controls load on primary and replicas
- Binlog/replica streaming: ensures zero data loss during copy
- Hooks and cutover control: operators can pre-check and abort safely
- Automatic replica lag monitoring: pauses copy when lag grows beyond threshold

## Before / After (Representative numbers)
| Metric | Before (ALTER TABLE) | After (gh-ost) |
|--------|----------------------:|---------------:|
| Lock duration on primary | minutes → hours | <200 ms (atomic RENAME window) |
| Replication lag spikes | minutes to tens of seconds | kept <1-2s by throttling |
| User-visible downtime | possible (writes blocked) | 0 (no blocking) |
| Migrations per week (operationally feasible) | only during maintenance windows | many, in regular deploy flow |

> Note: GitHub has used gh-ost on tables with tens-to-hundreds of millions of rows — avoiding long locks entirely and letting engineers run migrations as part of normal operations.

## Why this worked (technical reasons)
- Copying data off the critical fast path avoids long locks.
- Applying binlog changes ensures eventual consistency and no data loss.
- The final rename is atomic in MySQL and is the only short window where metadata changes — keeping user impact negligible.
- Built-in throttling prevents replication lag cascades that would otherwise amplify problems.

## Results & Impact
- Eliminated planned maintenance windows for many schema changes.
- Dramatically reduced risk: long-running DDLs and replication storms became rare.
- Allowed more frequent and safer schema evolution; migrations that used to require coordination and calendar slots became runbooked background jobs.
- Operators can migrate very large tables (hundreds of millions of rows) without taking services offline.

## Operational lessons
- Always monitor replica lag; set conservative thresholds for production.
- Use hooks to validate data post-cutover (feature flags, consistency checks).
- Throttle aggressively during business-critical hours; run bulk work in low-traffic windows when possible.

## Visual checklist for running gh-ost in prod
- Have a primary + replicas with binlog enabled (ROW-based is preferred)
- Ensure a replication topology that gh-ost can read from
- Define safe thresholds: replica lag, chunk size, QPS throttle
- Prepare rollback/runbook for failed cutover

## Final thought
gh-ost turned a painful, risky operational problem into a repeatable low-risk process. The pattern is simple: avoid blocking the primary, keep a shadow copy in sync, and swap atomically.
keyTakeaways: Avoid long metadata locks: copy and catch-up via binlog, then swap with atomic RENAME (lock window reduced from minutes/hours to <200ms).
Throttling + replica-lag monitoring is crucial: keep replicas healthy by pausing copy when lag exceeds safe thresholds (keeps lag in the ~1s range instead of minutes).
Operationalize schema changes: with tools like gh-ost, large-table migrations become routine — you can do more frequent schema evolution with lower risk.

concept.title: Stop the Cascade: How the Circuit Breaker Pattern Keeps Services Healthy
concept.summary: When a downstream service starts failing, naive retries or blind traffic can bring the whole system down. The Circuit Breaker pattern detects repeated failures, stops hammering the unhealthy component, and gives it time to recover — preventing cascading outages and improving system resilience.
concept.explanation:
## Why it matters

Imagine a popular API depends on a payment gateway. If the gateway becomes slow or starts failing, every request that waits or retries can pile up, exhausting threads, queues, and DB connections — and then the API itself becomes unavailable. The **Circuit Breaker** acts like an electrical breaker or a bouncer: when too many failures happen, it *opens* and stops calls to the failing component so the rest of the system stays healthy.

### Analogy

- *Electrical circuit breaker*: when current is too high, the breaker trips to prevent damage.
- *Nightclub bouncer*: if a group starts a fight, the bouncer temporarily blocks them from entering to stop the disturbance from escalating.

Both prevent one failing element from bringing everything else down.

---

## How it works (conceptual)

There are three primary states:

- **Closed**: calls are allowed normally. Failures are counted.
- **Open**: calls fail-fast (or return fallback) immediately. After a timeout, it moves to *Half-Open*.
- **Half-Open**: allow a limited number of trial calls. If they succeed, the breaker *closes* (normal operation). If they fail, the breaker *opens* again.

### Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
  Client[Client Request] --> CB{Circuit Breaker}
  CB -->|Closed: forward| Downstream[Downstream Service]
  Downstream -->|Success| CB2[Reset failure counter]
  Downstream -->|Failure| CB3[Increment failure counter]
  CB -->|Open: fail-fast| Fallback[Fallback / Error response]
  CB3 -->|Threshold reached| OpenState[Open - start timeout]
  OpenState -->|Timeout expires| HalfOpen[Half-Open: trial call]
  HalfOpen -->|Trial success| ClosedState[Close breaker]
  HalfOpen -->|Trial failure| OpenState
```

### Sequence diagram (common request path)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
  Client->>API: Request
  API->>CircuitBreaker: execute(call)
  CircuitBreaker->>Downstream: invoke
  Downstream-->>CircuitBreaker: success / failure
  CircuitBreaker-->>API: result or fallback
  API-->>Client: response
```

---

## Before / After: What changes?

| Without Circuit Breaker | With Circuit Breaker | When to Use |
|-------------------------|----------------------|-------------|
| Requests queue up behind a slow/failed service, causing thread / connection exhaustion | Failing calls short-circuit quickly; resources spared for healthy work | When downstream failures or latency can cascade (DB, external APIs, third-party services) |
| Blind retries multiply the load on an already failing service | Retries are controlled and usually combined with backoff and breaker state | Use when you can tolerate a fallback or transient degraded functionality |
| Hard to reason about tail latency spikes | Latency becomes more predictable because failed dependencies are rejected fast | Systems with SLAs or limited resources per component |

---

## Practical example and code

Key parameters to configure:

- **Failure threshold**: e.g., 5 failures within a sliding window
- **Reset timeout**: how long the breaker stays open before trying again
- **Half-open trial count**: how many requests to allow through in half-open
- **Which errors count**: timeouts, 5xx, network errors — not always 404s or 400s

### Minimal JavaScript circuit breaker (illustrative)

```javascript
class CircuitBreaker {
  constructor({failureThreshold = 5, recoveryTimeout = 10000, halfOpenMax = 2} = {}) {
    this.failureThreshold = failureThreshold
    this.recoveryTimeout = recoveryTimeout
    this.halfOpenMax = halfOpenMax

    this.failures = 0
    this.state = 'CLOSED' // CLOSED, OPEN, HALF_OPEN
    this.nextAttempt = 0
    this.halfOpenAttempts = 0
  }

  async execute(action) {
    const now = Date.now()
    if (this.state === 'OPEN') {
      if (now >= this.nextAttempt) {
        this.state = 'HALF_OPEN'
        this.halfOpenAttempts = 0
      } else {
        throw new Error('Circuit open: fail-fast')
      }
    }

    try {
      const result = await action()
      this._onSuccess()
      return result
    } catch (err) {
      this._onFailure()
      throw err
    }
  }

  _onSuccess() {
    this.failures = 0
    if (this.state === 'HALF_OPEN') this.state = 'CLOSED'
  }

  _onFailure() {
    this.failures += 1
    if (this.state === 'HALF_OPEN' || this.failures >= this.failureThreshold) {
      this.state = 'OPEN'
      this.nextAttempt = Date.now() + this.recoveryTimeout
    }
  }
}

// Usage:
// const cb = new CircuitBreaker({failureThreshold: 3, recoveryTimeout: 5000})
// await cb.execute(() => fetch('https://payments.example/charge'))
```

Notes:
- This is a simplified illustration. Production implementations handle sliding windows, metrics, and async race conditions.
- Prefer battle-tested libraries: Resilience4j (Java), Polly (C#), opossum (Node.js).

---

## When to use Circuit Breaker

- You depend on remote services (3rd party APIs, internal services, DB) that can fail or become slow.
- You want to avoid cascading failures and make system behavior more predictable.
- You can accept degraded behavior or fallbacks (e.g., stale cache, queued work, error responses) instead of blocking.

### When NOT to use

- For trivial, local synchronous calls that can't fail in ways that impact system resources.
- If you can't provide a meaningful fallback and a fail-fast error is worse than waiting — though even then, controlled waits + timeouts are better than uncontrolled retries.

---

## Common pitfalls

- **Counting the wrong errors**: Treating 400-series client errors as failures inflates counts unnecessarily. Only count errors that indicate a problem for the client (timeouts, 5xx, network failures).
- **Too-sensitive thresholds**: If thresholds are too low, the breaker opens on harmless blips, causing unnecessary degradation.
- **Long open windows**: If the open timeout is too long, recovery is delayed; too short and you risk retry storms.
- **No observability**: If you don't emit metrics (state transitions, failure counts, rejected calls), you won't know why things are failing.
- **Combined with naive retries**: Retrying aggressively when the breaker is half-open or closed can re-flood a recovering service — pair with exponential backoff and jitter.

---

## Operational recommendations

- Emit metrics: failure_count, state (CLOSED/OPEN/HALF_OPEN), rejected_calls
- Add logs on state transitions for postmortems
- Use rolling windows or exponential moving averages for failure rates rather than simple counters
- Combine with retries + exponential backoff and with the **Bulkhead** pattern to prevent resource exhaustion
- Test with fault injection (e.g., chaos engineering) to validate behavior

---

## TL;DR

Circuit Breakers prevent a failing dependency from taking the whole system down by failing fast and allowing controlled recovery. Tune thresholds, collect metrics, and combine with retries/backoff and bulkheads for robust resilience.
keyTakeaways: Circuit breakers protect systems from cascading failures by failing fast when a downstream service misbehaves.
Tune thresholds, timeouts, and which errors count; pair breakers with retries/backoff and observability.
Use production libraries (Resilience4j, Polly, opossum) and test behavior with fault injection.
